facts: During cellular respiration, glucose and oxygen react to produce ATP, water, and carbon dioxide; the carbon dioxide is then exhaled from the lungs.
The glucose used in cellular respiration comes from the digestion of carbohydrates; without digestion, your cells would not have glucose for energy.
Oxygen is required for cellular respiration; without it, cells cannot efficiently produce ATP from glucose, and much less energy is available.
ATP (adenosine triphosphate) is the main energy carrier in cells; when a cell needs energy, it splits off one phosphate group from ATP, releasing energy for tasks like muscle contraction.
Enzymes are proteins that speed up metabolic reactions without being used up; for example, the enzyme amylase in saliva begins breaking down starch into sugars during digestion.
Metabolism includes all chemical reactions happening in your body, both building up molecules (like making proteins) and breaking them down (like cellular respiration).
misconceptions: Students believe that cellular respiration happens in the lungs because we breathe in oxygen and out carbon dioxide, but it actually occurs in every cell.
Students think that the energy from food is used directly by cells, without understanding that it must first be converted into ATP.
Students think that enzymes are consumed or destroyed during metabolic reactions, so the body must constantly replace them, but enzymes are recycled and reusable.
